sentences.text: Well, it was the main trial.
And then after that, I had to work the trial of the SS, Oswald Pohl, and also, [GERMAN].
And I was yanked, also, to participate in the trial of Krupp, to help out there.
So sometimes, we were called to make spot translation or to assist in the interrogation of witnesses.
I was at the interrogation of a witness who was in Auschwitz.
He was a, a, a German electrical engineer, but he was anti-Hitler, and he belonged to the German Democratic Party.
And since he was an opponent of Hitler, he was arrested and sent to Auschwitz.
In fact, he showed us the tattoo, you know, that they have on their hand.
And he was ready to, to be gassed and killed.
He was already undressing and getting ready to be killed.
And suddenly, he was told to put his clothes on and see the commander of Auschwitz.
The others were killed.
He was suddenly saved at the last minute.
And the commander of Auschwitz says, you are an electrical engineer, aren't you?
He says, yeah, that's my profession.
Well, we need you.
What happened in Auschwitz is that many people found out that it was not a transfer camp to resettlement in the east, but it was a death camp.
So many people that were in line to be executed, they, they thought to, to end it all by running and jumping on the barbed wire, which had high electric tension.
So they would be instantly killed, but you had a short circuit.
And the young SS men did not really know how to repair this.
And so therefore, his job was to repair this short-- so his hair was all white.
I mean, he, he saw this.
Because he saw that if a man was running towards the barbed wire to commit suicide, the young SS men would shoot them in the stomach.
And that is a most painful wound.
And they would let them lie on the ground.
Nobody was allowed to touch him or to help him.
And he would bleed to death, and pain is terrific.
And his cries, according to the German theory, was that he would discourage others to commit suicide, that they would meekly go into the gas chamber to be killed.
So, the job-- but we did cooperate.
If we needed some information that some other trial had, we always did give-- would visit the lawyer and get the necessary information to, to explain.
Because there is always a background to the documents that you have, and you have to find the-- so all these trials were going at the same time, I guess the lawyers against the doctors.
And we would cooperate.
And sometimes, we would work on one trial or the other.
But we did have a job to work on our trial.
[LAUGHS] That was our main job, naturally.
Well, we had very nice rooms.
And we did have the necessary equipment to work with.
So the conditions were-- the only thing is that, naturally, you had to go through security to get into the courthouse.
In the beginning, we were afraid that the Germans might stage an effort to free the criminals.
And so in the beginning, we did have even tanks protecting the entrance to the courthouse.
We had to have special passes that would be checked, and there was a photograph on the pass so that they would see that you are-- [LAUGHS] --who you are.
So it was strict security, definitely.
One thing that happened is that when this fellow, the commander of Auschwitz, was testifying, we did have court stenographers, girls that would take notes.
And when he went into detail about the horrors of Auschwitz, some of those girls broke into tears and couldn't do-- continue the work.
So we had to call in some soldiers to replace those girls, because the details were such-- so horrible.
They were definitely.
So we had to use that.
We had-- it was interesting.
We had the personal interrupter of Hitler, who was hired by War Crimes because he spoke so many languages.
And he did have a secretary.
He was quite pompous.
And one day I was passing his office, and his office was empty.
And I start asking, what happened to Dr.
Schmidt?
He is not here.
They says, well, we had to check on him.
And we found out that he was involved in the murder of General [FRENCH] of the French army, who was a POW.
He was involved in that murder.
And so he's in prison now.
So from war crime trials, he went to prison.
[LAUGHS] Because they found.
We did have a survivor of Holocaust working in our office.
And he fell in love with a beautiful blonde girl.
And we were telling him that she is a Nazi.
How could he, a survivor of the Holocaust, go with a Nazi girl, even if she was cute?
Well, he says, she's not a Nazi.
I said, how do you know?
We have documents that she was a fuhrer of [GERMAN], and she was definitely a member of the Nazis.
Oh, she told me so.
I was really amazed at, at that.
I know that our military government, to teach democracy to Germans, was a complete failure.
Because as we occupied Germany, they would pick up a young lieutenant from Texas, right?
And say, well, you are in charge of this village, and you have to de-Nazify them.
Naturally, this fellow didn't know any German.
So he would meet the whole group, the inhabitants.
And he says anybody who knows English?
Well, a cute girl says yes, I speak English.
OK.
You will be my interpreter.
So he says, we have to choose a non-Nazi to be the mayor of the village.
Let's see.
Do we have anybody who is not a Nazi?
So the girl says, well, my uncle is not a Nazi.
You know, you could choose him.
So he appointed her uncle.
He says, we have to have a police chief.
Well, my father is quite [INAUDIBLE].
And later on, what they found out, they were the biggest Nazis that he appointed.
And Washington definitely opened a school here in Washington, you know, to teach people German and German history and so on to replace these failures.
And that was an innocent failure, you know, but it did not teach-- [LAUGHS] --democracy that way.
You can't teach democracy that way.
[LAUGHS]
Well, we were young, and we were enthusiastic that we are doing a job that has to be done.
And we were quite optimistic about the future.
So that, that was the spirit, that it has to be done because it has not been done before.
And we thought it would be a benefit for the humanity that would deter people.
Just like now, we think that maybe death sentence would deter people from murder and policemen and so on.
We thought, too, that maybe Nuremberg would deter from atrocities and that people would behave in a civilized manner.
We thought that maybe something could be done.
Because I still believe that not everybody is bad.
I mean, you do have some decent, decent people, only they don't get the headlines.
[LAUGHS] The bad people do.
Well, we usually would get around the table and discuss our activities, share our experiences.
We had one fellow from Brooklyn who very much wanted to take a picture with Colonel Orlov of the Soviet delegation.
And Colonel Orlov said nothing doing.
I can't take a picture with you.
So he suddenly says, well, you know, comes the revolution in the United States, if I have a picture with you, I can say I was a friend of Colonel Orlov.
Oh, he says.
In that case, let's take the picture.
And he got the picture.
[LAUGHS]
So we had some very interesting experiences, you know?
One thing-- some witnesses would be with us in the restaurant.
Like, there was a very beautiful Polish girl, and she was a messenger of the underground, anti-Nazi underground in Warsaw.
And she had to carry messages to different units of the underground.
And she was caught by the Gestapo.
She was able to swallow the message, but they knew that she was a courier.
And they thought that maybe they would discover the organization of the underground.
And they tortured her and so on, but they couldn't get anything, because the underground was definitely divided into-- they were-- they had safety.
So they took her to Ravensbruck concentration camp where they conducted medical experiments.
And she had pretty legs, and so they decided to have a multiple fracture of her legs without any anesthesia.
She was strapped to a table, and they have multiple fracture of her legs.
And they would use different methods to try to cure her, and they, they were very proud.
They said, oh, we contributed something to medicine because we found out that the steel rod does mend the bones.
But they didn't even bother to sew her.
So when she showed her legs, they were a horrible, you know, mess.
And-- but she was very pretty.
She was very popular.
[LAUGHS] But it shows you that they did conduct experiments.
And actually, the Germans lawyers told us, well, you did commit some atrocities, too.
They says, Marines in the Pacific and Guadalcanal, they used to cut the ears of the Japanese and wearing them on their belt.
But the difference is that our atrocities were not planned by our government.
They were spontaneous.
People facing horrors of people smeared with honey and being killed by the Japanese, and so on, so that was a reaction, and it was not here.
All the experiments that the Nazis committed were planned by the government.

So that was a difference between the Allies and between-- in fact, when we had training for interrogation of prisoners of war, we thought that the only way to make a, a German confess is to bring the biggest fellow and say that he is a Pole whose family was killed by the Germans.
And we would leave you with him, you know?
He has a score to settle, and we will leave the tent.
The German would get so scared, he would spill the beans.
[LAUGHS]
So we didn't use any.
[CLEARS THROAT]
[LAUGHS] Or for example, the SS outfit that killed American prisoners of war after the Battle of Bastogne, the Germans filmed that atrocity.
And they showed the Americans lined up, their hands tied, and being executed by the SS.
And then the SS would march to them and get the Chesterfield cigarettes, you know, and smile to the camera.
Well, we caught these murderers.
We put them in prison in Belgium, in Brussels.
And the trial naturally condemned them to be hanged.
Because we did have the film, we could identify them.
The German general asked not to be hanged.
He wanted to be shot.
And we said, no.
We can't do that.
We have to hang you.
So some of them didn't want to confess.
So what we did is we told the Belgians who suffered under the German occupation that we would leave the prison.
There would be no guards, nothing, you know?
And people stormed the prison and gave the Germans a thorough beating.
We didn't have anything to do with it.
The guards came in, rifles, so on.
And after that, they says, would you like another visit?
[LAUGHS] They confessed.
[LAUGHS] And they were hanged for their horrible crime.
They were-- but you see, it was not planned by our government.
It was done individually, if you could call it a war crime.
It's not a, a conscious plan.
Definitely not.
So that, that was the excuse.
Well, there were a lot of Germans that would give me an argument that the Nuremberg trial is a lie, is a-- Allied propaganda.
It is not true.
It couldn't have happened.
So our public relations did show some on the radio.
They had the proceedings translated into German.
They tried to show films too so that they would-- they would see.
Because many people did not believe it.
They couldn't believe that such a Western nation, Western civilization, would do things like that.
Many people were very skeptical about that.
In fact, I know we, we were helping the German population.
So food was brought in, because there were shortages of food.
And we always liked to dress in civilian, take off our uniform and in civilian to go to a German pub and have some beer, and sometimes listen to the conversation of those Bavarians, you know?
And so we marched into a pub, we got a table, we got our beer.
Right next to us, there was a group of Bavarian sitting.
And one in a loud voice would say, hey, did you see that American propaganda?
They says, what? what? what?
They says, did you-- did you know that that butter that they brought here to bring it to us had American wrapping, but it was really the German butter?
[LAUGHS] They were skeptical of our foreign aid.
Then they looked upon us and they suddenly switched the conversation, and we didn't know why.
Because the American clothing cut is different than the German cut.
Even the shoes are different.
So they looked at us.
Even if we did speak German, you know, they knew.
[LAUGHS] And so they switched the conversation.
But that was very interesting, you know, to listen to some of the comments.
[LAUGHS] They didn't even believe in the foreign aid that was coming in to feed them.
It's really amazing.
Yeah.
Well, some of the trials were over.
And they needed reviewers to review the, the material that was printed in many, many volumes.
You know, the Nuremberg proceedings are available.
They were printed.
And we did have to review.
So they asked me to fill in as a reviewer.
I was working as a reviewer through the different trials.
We did have a lot of journalists in Nuremberg.
They were sitting-- they were living in the palace of Faber, you know, the one that makes pencils?
Yeah.
That was the headquarters of the press there.
I was invited down there.
It was interesting to meet press from all different countries.
We did even have-- I remember meeting a Bulgarian newspaperman.
And another thing, we did discuss the crime committed in the Katyn Forest.
You probably have heard about that crime, where officers were executed by the Soviet secret police.
There was a Bulgarian doctor as a witness at the Nuremberg trial saying that he did visit the Katyn forest.
And the strange thing that he said-- he said that the Polish officers that were killed there, some of them had summer uniforms.
The others had winter uniforms.
I don't know what that means.
I mean, that was not picked up by, by the press or by anybody else.
But you see, during the summer, it was the advance of the German-- I mean, the Germans were advancing, but it was still under Soviet control.
In winter, it was already under German control.
So some people theorized that the Germans didn't find enough corpses there, so they added some new ones.
I don't know whether this is true or not, but that, that was interesting that the Katyn Forest came up during the Nuremberg trial.
It did, did come up.
And many other trials we, we, we did discuss, too, you know?
Did you stay in Nuremberg well after the end of the trial?
Or when did you leave Nuremberg?
No.
When my trial was over, I was working as a reviewer.
And I didn't stay any longer, because I had to go back to school.
And when I came back to the States, I saw a big sign in Times Square-- two horror hits, Boris Karloff and Nuremberg trials.
And the American colleges didn't have any room for me, because it was right after the war, you know?
Many veterans.
And they say you can go to Sarah Lawrence College.
They have an opening there.
Oh, a girls college.
Sarah Lawrence.
But then I went to Columbia, and I say, if I get a degree from Sarah Lawrence, would you accept me for graduate studies?
Uh uh.
We won't.
The BA from Sarah Lawrence is not valid here at Columbia.
You will have to take another year.
And I say, hey.
I'm not going to waste my time.
So I start applying overseas, and two universities were considering me.
One was the University of Edinburgh, Scotland, and then the University of Basel in Switzerland.
And Switzerland was the one that accepted me right away, and I went to Switzerland to study with a whole group of Americans.
And we went by ship to Antwerp.
And from Antwerp, we had to go by train.
Being students, we bought the cheapest transportation-- third class, hard seats.
And we were sitting, a group of us, and we heard somebody yelling, this is an outrage.
I have a ticket for second class, and you put me in the third class.
They says, that's the only place we have room.
And in walks, walks a French major, mad as hell.
Of course, and then he listens to us speaking English.
He says, oh, you are English?
No, we are Americans.
Oh, Americans.
Ah, Americans always travel first class.
What are you doing here?
I say, well, we are students.
[LAUGHS] We have to watch our expenses.
He thought American always travel first class.
[LAUGHS] He saw Americans vacationing in Europe, you know, traveling first class.
Well, I stayed till, till '48 in Nuremberg, and then, then I went back home.
And since I couldn't get there, then I went to Switzerland in '48.
Yeah.
One year, because then I had to return home.
And I went to UCLA.
[LAUGHS]
Well, my mother was offered a job in-- at UCLA in the Slavic department.
And so I went with her to LA, where I studied until 1950.
And then I went to graduate studies to Columbia.
So I lived in California.
[LAUGHS]
Well, my interest was naturally in Eastern Europe since I grew up in the Kingdom of Romania and I speak their languages.
And I know the Church Slavonic, too, which is very popular in Bulgaria, Yugoslavia, Serbia.
And so I specialized in East European studies, which would be also the relationship of Eastern Europe with Russia.
Since I know the Russian language, I can get to primary source materials.
And so my contribution is that material, primary source materials, that have never been translated into English, I translate them into, into English.
And that is my contribution, like chronicles and how the people feel there in that area, which we should know a lot more.
Then I studied, also, Byzantine studies.
Because in order to understand Eastern Europe, you have to know the Byzantine Empire.
And I studied that, too.
Greek comes in handy, and Church Slavonic definitely comes in handy.
In fact, Constantinople used to be called by the Slav Tsarigrad, the City of the Caesars, Tsarigrad which a lot of people don't know about.
In the documents, suddenly you see Tsarigrad.
Which city are they talking about?
They're talking about Constantinople.
So many of these things, I, I do bring up that way.
And I know some people dismiss Eastern Europe.
Eastern Europe is not important.
Western Europe is important.
This is what one of my colleagues said, you know?
And I said, well.
In fact, the dictator of Romania, Marshal Antonescu, he protected the Romanian Jews, because the Germans wanted to send them to concentration camps to be killed.
And Marshal Antonescu says, no.
They are our Romanian Jews, and you can't touch them.
So the only thing that happened to the Jews that they were in work groups working.
But they were decently treated, and they were allowed later on to immigrate to Israel.
Many, many of my friends are in Israel.
Another thing that happened in Eastern Europe was that General Francisco Franco, the leader of Spain-- there are many Jews in Eastern Europe that still speak Spanish, you know?
During Isabella, they fled.
And they use the Spanish of Cervantes, you know, Spanish.
And when I was in Spanish [? litigation ?] working in the library there in order to learn Spanish, because I didn't have money to pay for lessons in Spanish, so I worked in the library there.
And there was a Jewish rabbi who came with a group of these Sephardi Jews.
And in the old Spanish language of Cervantes, he thanked the ambassador, Spanish ambassador, Condado de Casa Rojas, for saving the Jews by giving them Spanish passports.
And some of them were able to immigrate that way with a Spanish passport.
In fact, my roommate in Madrid was a Sephardi Jew who had the-- who was in the dry cleaning business.
And he was in Madrid waiting for a ship to go to Venezuela.
He got a visa to Venezuela, and he's now in the diamond business.
[LAUGHS] He was able to escape.
So a lot of people don't know about that, you know, that some people were saved that way.
In Hungary, it was quite different.
The poor people of Transylvania, they suffered a lot.
Because in 1940, Transylvania was divided into two parts.
One part was given to Hungary.
The other one remained with Romania.
And I know a professor from the University of California who was a kid who lived in the part that belonged to Hungary.
And he and his family were sent to Auschwitz while the other Jews in Romania, they were saved.
So a lot, lot of interesting things that happened there.
And-- OK.
Sure.
